html:
X

Towards the end of June there were frequent excursions.

Into all the gatherings at Waldstra√Яe the outside world came like a presence. It removed the sense of pressure, of being confronted and challenged. Everything that was said seemed to be incidental to it, like remarks dropped in a low tone between individuals at a great conference.

Miriam wondered again and again whether her companions shared this sense with her. Sometimes when they were all sitting together she longed to ask, to find out, to get some public acknowledgment of the magic that lay over everything. At times it seemed as if could they all be still for a momentвБ†вАФit must take shape. It was everywhere, in the food, in the fragrance rising from the opened lid of the tea urn, in all the needful unquestioned movements, the requests, the handings and thanks, the going from room to room, the partings and assemblings. It hung about the fabrics and fittings of the house. Overwhelmingly it came in through oblongs of window giving on to stairways. Going upstairs in the light pouring in from some uncurtained window, she would cease for a moment to breathe.

Whenever she found herself alone she began to sing, softly. When she was with others a head drooped or lifted, the movement of a hand, the light falling along the detail of a profile could fill her with happiness.

It made companionship a perpetual question. At rare moments there would come a tingling from head to foot, a faint buzzing at her lips and at the tip of each finger. At these moments she could raise her eyes calmly to those about her and drink in the fact of their presence, see them all with perfect distinctness, but without distinguishing one from the other. She wanted to say, вАЬIsnвАЩt it extraordinary? Do you realise?вАЭ She felt that if only she could make her meaning clear all difficulties must vanish. Outside in the open, going forward to some goal through sunny mornings, gathering at inns, wading through the scented undergrowth of the woods, she would dream of the secure return to Waldstra√Яe, their own beleaguered place. She saw it opening out warm and familiar back and back to the strange beginning in the winter. They would be there again tonight, singing.

One morning she knew that there was going to be a change. The term was coming to an end. There was to be a going away. The girls were talking about вАЬNorderney.вАЭ

вАЬGoing to Norderney, Hendy?вАЭ Jimmie said suddenly.

вАЬAh!вАЭ she responded mysteriously. For the rest of that day she sat contracted and fearful.

вАЬYou shall write and enquire of your good parents what they would have you do. You shall tell them that the German pupils return all to their homes; that the English pupils go for a happy holiday to the sea.вАЭ

вАЬOh yes,вАЭ said Miriam conversationally, with trembling breath.

вАЬIt is of course evident that since you will have no duties to perform, I cannot support the expense of your travelling and your maintenance.вАЭ

вАЬOh no, of course not,вАЭ said Miriam, her hands pressed against her knee.

She sat shivering in the warm dim Saal shaded by the close sun blinds. It looked as she had seen it with her father for the first time and Fr√§ulein sitting near seemed to be once more in the heavy panniered blue velvet dress.

She waited stiff and ugly till Fr√§ulein, secure and summer clad, spoke softly again.

вАЬYou think, my child, you shall like the profession of a teacher?вАЭ

вАЬOh yes,вАЭ said Miriam, from the midst of a tingling flush.

вАЬI think you have many qualities that make the teacher.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ You are earnest and serious-minded.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Grave.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Sometimes perhaps overgrave for your years.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But you have a serious faultвБ†вАФwhich must be corrected if you wish to succeed in your calling.вАЭ

Miriam tried to pull her features into an easy enquiring seriousness. A darkness was threatening her. вАЬYou have a most unfortunate manner.вАЭ

Without relaxing, Miriam quivered. She felt the blood mount to her head.

вАЬYou must adopt a quite, quite different manner. Your influence is, I think, good, a good English influence in its most general effect. But it is too slightly so and of too much indirection. You must exert it yourself, in a manner more alive, you must make it your aim that you shall have a responsible influence, a direct personal influence. You have too much of chill and formality. It makes a stiffness that I am willing to believe you do not intend.вАЭ

Miriam felt a faint dizziness.

вАЬIf you should fail to become more genial, more simple and natural as to your bearing, you will neither make yourself understood nor will you be loved by your pupils.вАЭ

вАЬNoвБ†вАФвАЭ responded Miriam, assuming an air of puzzled and interested consideration of Fr√§uleinвАЩs words. She was recovering. She must get to the end of the interview and get away and find the answer. Far away beneath her fear and indignation, Fr√§ulein was answered. She must get away and say the answer to herself.

вАЬTo truly fulfil the most serious role of the teacher you must enter into the personality of each pupil and must sympathise with the struggles of each one upon the path on which our feet are set. Efforts to good kindliness and thought for others must be encouraged. The teacher shall be sunshine, human sunshine, encouraging all effort and all lovely things in the personality of the pupil.вАЭ

Fr√§ulein rose and stood, tall. Then her half-tottering decorous footsteps began. Miriam had hardly listened to her last words. She felt tears of anger rising and tried to smile.

вАЬI shall say now no more. But when you shall hear from your good parents, we can further discuss our plans.вАЭ Fr√§ulein was at the door.

Fr√§ulein left the Saal by the small door and Miriam felt her way to the schoolroom. The girls were gathering there ready for a walk. Some were in the hall and Fr√§uleinвАЩs voice was giving instructions: вАЬMachen Sie schnell, Miss Henderson,вАЭ she called.

Fr√§ulein had never before called to her like that. It had always been as if she did not see her but assumed her ready to fall in with the general movements.

Now it was Fr√§ulein calling to her as she might do to Gertrude or Solomon. There was no hurried whisper from Jimmie telling her to вАЬfly for her life.вАЭ

вАЬJa, Fr√§ulein,вАЭ she cried gaily and blundered towards the basement stairs. Mademoiselle was standing averted at the head of them; Miriam glanced at her. Her face was red and swollen with crying.

The sight amazed Miriam. She considered the swollen suffusion under the large black hat as she ran downstairs. She hoped Mademoiselle did not see her glance.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Mademoiselle, standing there all disfigured and blotchy about somethingвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ it was nothingвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ it couldnвАЩt be anything.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ If anyone were dead she would not be standing thereвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ it was just some silly prim French quirkвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ her dignityвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ someone had been вАЬgrossi√®reвАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and there she stood in her black hat and black cotton gloves.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Hurriedly putting on her hat and long lace scarf she decided that she would not change her shoes. Somewhere out in the sunshine a hurdy-gurdy piped out the air of вАЬDass du mich liebst, das wusstвАЩ ich.вАЭ She glanced at the frosted barred window through which the dim light came into the dressing room. The piping notes, out of tune, wrongly emphasised, slurring one into the other, followed her across the dark basement hall and came faintly to her as she went slowly upstairs. There was no hurry. Everyone was talking busily in the hall, drowning the sound of her footsteps. She had forgotten her gloves. She went back into the cool grey musty rooms. A little crack in an upper pane shone like a gold thread. The barrel organ piped. As she stooped to gather up her gloves from the floor she felt the cold stone firm and secure under her hand. And the house stood up all round her with its rooms and the light lying along stairways and passages, and outside the bright hot sunshine and the roadways leading in all directions, out into Germany.

How could Fr√§ulein possibly think she could afford to go to Norderney? They would all go. Things would go on. She could not go thereвБ†вАФnor back to England. It was cruelвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ just torture and worry againвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ with the bright house all round herвБ†вАФthe high rooms, the dark old pianos, strange old garret, the unopened door beyond it. No help anywhere.

As they walked she laughed and talked with the girls, responding excitedly to all that was said. They walked along a broad and almost empty boulevard in two rows of four and five abreast, with Mademoiselle and Judy bringing up the rear. The talk was general and there was much laughter. It was the kind of interchange that arose when they were all together and there was anything вАЬin the air,вАЭ the kind that Miriam most disliked. She joined in it feverishly. ItвАЩs perfectly natural that they should all be excited about the holidays she told herself, stifling her thoughts. But it must not go too far. They wanted to be jolly.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ If I could be jolly too they would like me. I must not be a wet blanket.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ MademoiselleвАЩs voice was not heard. Miriam felt that the steering of the conversation might fall to anyone. Mademoiselle was extinguished. She must exert her influence. Presently she forgot MademoiselleвАЩs presence altogether. They were all walking along very quickly.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ If she were going to Norderney with the English girls she must be on easy terms with them.

вАЬAh, ha!вАЭ somebody was saying.

вАЬOhвАСho!вАЭ said Miriam in response.

вАЬIhвАСhi!вАЭ came another voice.

вАЬTreвАСlaвАСla,вАЭ trilled Bertha Martin gently.

вАЬYou mean TurrahвАСlaheeвАСtee,вАЭ said Miriam.

вАЬGood for you, Hendy,вАЭ blared Gertrude, in a swinging middle tone.

вАЬChalk it up. Chalk it up, children,вАЭ giggled Jimmie.

Millie looked pensively about her with vague disapproval. Her eyebrows were up. It seemed as if anything might happen; as if at any moment they might all begin running in different directions.

вАЬCave, my dear brats, be artig,вАЭ came BerthaвАЩs cool even tones.

вАЬAh! we are observed.вАЭ

вАЬNo, we are not observed. The observer observeth not.вАЭ

Miriam saw her companions looking across the boulevard.

Following their eyes she found the figure of Pastor Lahmann walking swiftly bag in hand in the direction of an opening into a side street.

вАЬAh!вАЭ she cried gaily. вАЬVoil√† Monsieur; courrez, Mademoiselle!вАЭ

At once she felt that it was cruel to draw attention to Mademoiselle when she was dumpy and upset.

вАЬWhat a fool I am,вАЭ she moaned in her mind. вАЬWhy canвАЩt I say the right thing?вАЭ

вАЬCe nвАЩest pas moi,вАЭ said Mademoiselle, вАЬqui fait les avances.вАЭ

The group walked on for a moment or two in silence. Bertha Martin was swinging her left foot out across the curb with each step, giving her right heel a little twirl to keep her balance.

вАЬYou are very clever BairвАСta,вАЭ said Mademoiselle, still in French, вАЬbut you will never make a prima ballerina.вАЭ

вАЬHulloh!вАЭ breathed Jimmie, вАЬsheвАЩs perking up.вАЭ

вАЬIsnвАЩt she,вАЭ said Miriam, feeling that she was throwing away the last shred of her dignity.

вАЬWhat was the matter?вАЭ she continued, trying to escape from her confusion.

MademoiselleвАЩs instant response to her cry at the sight of Pastor Lahmann rang in her ears. She blushed to the soles of her feet.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ How could Mademoiselle misunderstand her insane remark? What did she mean? What did she really think of her? Just kind old LahmannвБ†вАФwalking along there in the outside world.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ She did not want to stop him.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ He was a sort of kinsman for MademoiselleвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ that was what she had meant. Oh, why couldnвАЩt she get away from all these girls?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ indeedвБ†вАФand again she saw the hurrying figure which had disappeared leaving the boulevard with its usual effect of a great strange oceanвБ†вАФhe could have brought help and comfort to all of them if he had seen them and stopped. Pastor LahmannвБ†вАФLahmannвБ†вАФperhaps she would not see him again. Perhaps he could tell her what she ought to do.

вАЬOh, my dear,вАЭ Jimmie was saying, вАЬdidnвАЩt you know?вБ†вАФa fearful row.вАЭ

MademoiselleвАЩs laughter tinkled out from the rear.

вАЬA row?вАЭ

вАЬFearful!вАЭ JimmieвАЩs face came round, round-eyed under her white sailor hat that sat slightly tilted on the peak of her hair.

вАЬWhat about?вАЭ

вАЬSomething about a letter or something, or some letters or somethingвБ†вАФI donвАЩt know. Something she took out of the letter box, it was unlocked or something and Ulrica saw her and told Lily!вАЭ

вАЬGoodness!вАЭ breathed Miriam.

вАЬYes, and Lily had her in her room and Ulrica and poor little Petite couldnвАЩt deny it. Ulrica said she did nothing but cry and cry. SheвАЩs been crying all the morning, poor little pig.вАЭ

вАЬWhy did she want to take anything out of the box?вАЭ

вАЬOh, I donвАЩt know. There was a fearful row anyhow. Ulrica said Lily talked like a clergymanвБ†вАФwie ein Pfarrer.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I donвАЩt know. Ulrica said she was opening a letter. I donвАЩt know.вАЭ

вАЬBut she canвАЩt read German or English.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬI donвАЩt know. Ask me another.вАЭ

вАЬIt is extraordinary.вАЭ

вАЬWhatвАЩs extraordinary?вАЭ asked Bertha from the far side of Jimmie.

вАЬPetite and that letter.вАЭ

вАЬOh.вАЭ

вАЬWhat did the Kiddy want?вАЭ

вАЬOh, my dear, donвАЩt ask me to explain the peculiarities of the French temperament.вАЭ

вАЬYes, but all the letters in the letter box would be English or German, as Hendy says.вАЭ

Bertha glanced at Miriam. Miriam flushed. She could not discuss Mademoiselle with two of the girls at once.

вАЬRum go,вАЭ said Bertha.

вАЬYouвАЩre right, my son. ItвАЩs rum. ItвАЩs all over now, anyhow. ThereвАЩs no accounting for tastes. Poor old Petite.вАЭ

Miriam woke in the moonlight. She saw MademoiselleвАЩs face as it had looked at teatime, pale and cruel, silent and very old. Someone had said she had been in Fr√§uleinвАЩs room again all the afternoon.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Fr√§ulein had spoken to her once or twice during tea. She had answered coolly and eagerlyвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ disgustingвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ like a child that had been whipped and forgiven.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ How could Fr√§ulein dare to forgive anybody?

She lay motionless. The night was cool. The screens had not been moved. She felt that the door was shut. After a while she began in imagination a conversation with Eve.

вАЬYou see the trouble was,вАЭ she said and saw EveвАЩs downcast believing admiring sympathetic face, вАЬFr√§ulein talked to me about manner, she simply wanted me to grimace, simply. You knowвБ†вАФbe like other people.вАЭ

Eve laughed. вАЬYes, I know.вАЭ

вАЬYou see? Simply.вАЭ

вАЬWell, if you wanted to stay, why couldnвАЩt you?вАЭ

вАЬI simply couldnвАЩt; you know how people are.вАЭ

вАЬBut you can act so splendidly.вАЭ

вАЬBut you canвАЩt keep it up.вАЭ

вАЬWhy not?вАЭ

вАЬEve. There you are, you see, you always go back.вАЭ

вАЬI mean I think it would be simply lovely. If I were clever like you I should do it all the time, be simply always gushing and вАШcharming.вАЩвАКвАЭ

Then she reminded Eve of the day they had walked up the lane to the Heath talking over all the manners they would like to haveвБ†вАФand how Sarah suddenly in the middle of supper had caricatured the one they had chosen. вАЬOf course you overdid it,вАЭ she concluded, and Eve crimsoned and said, вАЬOh yes, I know it was my fault. But you could have begun all over again in Germany and been quite different.вАЭ

вАЬYes, I know I thought about that.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But if you knew as much of the world as I do.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

Eve stared, showing a faint resentment.

Miriam thought of EveвАЩs many suitors, of her six monthsвАЩ betrothal, of her lifelong peacemaking, her experiment in being governess to the two children of an artistвБ†вАФa little green-robed boy threatening her with a knife.

вАЬYes, but I mean if you had been about.вАЭ

вАЬI know,вАЭ smiled Eve confidently. вАЬYou mean if I were you. Go on. I know. Explain, old thing.вАЭ

вАЬWell, I mean of course if you are a governess in a school you canвАЩt be jolly and charming. You canвАЩt be idiotic or anything.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I did think about it. DonвАЩt tell anybody. But I thought for a little while I might go into a familyвБ†вАФone of the girlsвАЩ familiesвБ†вАФthe German girls, and begin having a German manner. Two of the girls asked me. One of them was ill and went awayвБ†вАФthat Pomeranian one I told you about. Well, then, I didnвАЩt tell you about that little one and her sisterвБ†вАФthey asked me to go to them for the holidays. The youngest saidвБ†вАФit was so absurdвБ†вАФвАШyou shall marry my bruzzerвБ†вАФhe is mairchantвБ†вАФvery weltyвАЩвБ†вАФabsurd.вАЭ

вАЬNot absurdвБ†вАФyou probably would have, away from that school.вАЭ

вАЬDвАЩyou think so?вАЭ

вАЬYes, you would have been a regular German, fat and jolly and laughing.вАЭ

вАЬI know. My dear I thought about it. You may imagine. I wondered if I ought.вАЭ

вАЬWhy didnвАЩt you try?вАЭ

Why not? Why was she not going to try? Eve would, she was sure in her place.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

Why not grimace and be very вАЬbrightвАЭ and вАЬanimatedвАЭ until the end of the term and then go and stay with the Bergmanns for two months and be as charming as she could?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Her heart sank.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ She imagined a house, everyone kind and blond and smiling. EmmaвАЩs big tall brother smiling and joking and liking her. She would laugh and pretend and flirt like the Pooles and make up to himвБ†вАФand it would be lovely for a little while. Then she would offend someone. She would offend everyone but EmmaвБ†вАФand get tired and cross and lose her temper. Stare at them all as they said the things everybody said, the things she hated; and she would sit glowering, and suddenly refuse to allow the women to be familiar with her.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ She tried to see the brother more clearly. She looked at the screen. The BergmannsвАЩ house would be full of German furniture.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ At the end of a week every bit of it would reproach her.

She tried to imagine him without the house and the family, not talking or joking or pretendingвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ alone and sadвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ despising his familyвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ needing her. He loved forests and music. He had a great strong solid voice and was strong and sure about everything and she need never worry any more.

вАЬSeit ich ihn gesehen
GlaubвАЩ ich blind zu sein.вАЭ

There would be a garden and German springs and summers and sunsets and strong kind arms and a shoulder. She would grow so happy. No one would recognise her as the same person. She would wear a band of turquoise-blue velvet ribbon round her hair and look at the mountains.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ No good. She could never get out to that. Never. She could not pretend long enough. Everything would be at an end long before there was any chance of her turning into a happy German woman.

Certainly with a German man she would be angry at once. She thought of the men she had seenвБ†вАФin the streets, in caf√©s and gardens, the masters in the school, photographs in the girlsвАЩ albums. They had all offended her at once. Something in their bearing and manner.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Blind and impudent.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ She thought of the interview she had witnessed between Ulrica and her cousinвБ†вАФthe cousin coming up from the estate in Erfurth, arriving in a carriage, Fr√§uleinвАЩs manner, her smiles and hints; Ulrica standing in the Saal in her sprigged saffron muslin dress curtseyingвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ with bent head, the cousinвАЩs condescending laughing voice. It would never do for her to go into a German home. She must not say anything about the chance of going to the BergmannsвАЩвБ†вАФeven to Eve.

She imagined Eve sitting listening in the window space in the bow that was carpeted with linoleum to look like parquet flooring. Beyond them lay the length of the Turkey carpet darkening away under the long table. She could see each object on the shining sideboard. The silver biscuit box and the large √©pergne made her feel guilty and shifting, guilty from the beginning of things.

вАЬYou see, Eve, I thought counting it all up that if I came home it would cost less than going to Norderney and that all the expense of my going to Germany and coming back is less than what it would have cost to keep me at home for the five months IвАЩve been thereвБ†вАФI wish youвАЩd tell everybody that.вАЭ

She turned about in bed; her head was growing fevered.

She conjured up a vision of the backs of the books in the bookcase in the dining room at home.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Iliad and OdysseyвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ people going over the sea in boats and someone doing embroideryвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ that little picture of Hector and Andromache in the corner of a pageвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ he in armourвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ she, in a trailing dress, holding up her baby. Both, silly.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ She wished she had read more carefully. She could not remember anything in Lecky or Darwin that would tell her what to doвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ HudibrasвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ The Atomic TheoryвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Ballads and Poems, D. G. RossettiвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ KinglakeвАЩs CrimeaвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ PalgraveвАЩs ArabiaвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Crimea.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ The Crimea.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Florence Nightingale; a picture somewhere; a refined face, with cap and strings.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ She must have smiled.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ MotleyвАЩs Rise ofвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Rise ofвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ MotleyвАЩs Rise of the Dutch Republic.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ MotleyвАЩs Rise of the Dutch Republic and the Chronicles of the Sch√ґnberg-Cotta Family. She held to the memory of these two books. Something was coming from them to her. She handled the shiny brown gold-tooled back of MotleyвАЩs Rise and felt the hard graining of the red-bound Chronicles.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ There were green trees outside in the moonlightвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ in LutherвАЩs GermanyвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ trees and fields and German towns and then Holland. She breathed more easily. Her eyes opened serenely. Tranquil moonlight lay across the room. It surprised her like a sudden hand stroking her brow. It seemed to feel for her heart. If she gave way to it her thoughts would go. Perhaps she ought to watch it and let her thoughts go. It passed over her trouble like her mother did when she said, вАЬDonвАЩt go so deeply into everything, chickie. You must learn to take life as it comes. AhвАСeh if I were strong I could show you how to enjoy life.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ Delicate little mother, running quickly downstairs clearing her throat to sing. But mother did not know. She had no reasoning power. She could not help because she did not know. The moonlight was sad and hesitating. Miriam closed her eyes again. LutherвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ pinning up that notice on a church door.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ (Why is Luther like a dyspeptic blackbird? Because the Diet of Worms did not agree with him)вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and then leaving the notice on the church door and going home to teaвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ coffeeвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ some evening mealвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ K√§theвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ K√§theвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ happy K√§the.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ They pinned up that notice on a Roman Catholic churchвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and all the priests looked at themвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and behind the priests were torture and dark placesвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Luther looking up to GodвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ saying you couldnвАЩt get away from your sins by paying moneyвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ standing out in the world and K√§the making the meal at homeвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Luther was fat and German. Perhaps his face perspiredвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Eine feste Burg; a firm fortressвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ a round tower made of old brown bricks and no windows.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ No need for K√§the to smile.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ She had been a nunвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and then making a lamplit meal for Luther in a wooden German houseвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and Rome waiting to kill them.

Darwin had come since then. There were peopleвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ distinguished minds, who thought Darwin was true.

No God. No Creation. The struggle for existence. Fighting.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Fighting.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Fighting.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Everybody groping and fighting.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Fr√§ulein.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Some said it was trueвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ some not. They could not both be right. It was probably trueвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ only old-fashioned people thought it was not. It was true. Just thatвБ†вАФmonkeys fighting. But who began it? Who made Fr√§ulein? Tough leathery monkey.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

Then nothing matters. Just one little short life.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

вАЬA few more years shall rollвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶
A few more seasons pass.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

There was a better one than thatвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ not so organ-grindery.

вАЬSwift to its close ebbs out lifeвАЩs little day;
EarthвАЩs joys grow dim, its glories fade away;
Change and decay in all around I see.вАЭ

Sighing impatiently she turned aboutвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and sighed again, breathing deeply and rattling and feeling very hungry.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ There will be breakfast, even for me.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ If they knew me they would not give me breakfast.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ No one wouldвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I should be in a little room and one after another would come and be reproachful and shockedвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and then they would go away and be happy and forget.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

Sarah would come. Whatever it was, Sarah would come. She read the Bible and marked pieces.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But she would rush in without saying anything, with a red face and bang down a plate of melon.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ What did God do about people like Sarah? Perhaps Apollyon could be made to come at onceвБ†вАФsweeping in like a large batвБ†вАФbe torn to bitsвБ†вАФthose men at that college said he had come to them. They sworeвБ†вАФone after the other and the devil came in through one of the carved windows and carried one of them away.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I have my doubtsвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ PaterвАЩs face laughingвБ†вАФI have my doubts, ooofвБ†вАФPвАСooof. She flung off the outer covering and felt the strong movements of her limbs. Hang! Hang! Hang! Damn.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

If thereвАЩs no God, thereвАЩs no DevilвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and everything goes on.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Fr√§ulein goes on having her school.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ What does she really think?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Out in the world people donвАЩt think.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ They grimace.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Is there anywhere where there are no people?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ be a gipsy.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ There are always people.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

вАЬWhat a perfect morningвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ what a perfect morning,вАЭ Miriam kept telling herself, trying to see into the garden. There was a bowl of irises on the breakfast tableвБ†вАФit made everything seem strange. There had never been flowers on the table before. There was also a great dish of pumpernickel besides the usual food. Fr√§ulein had enjoined silence. The silence made the impression of the irises stay. She hoped it might be a new rule. She glanced at Fr√§ulein two or three times. She was pallid white. Her face looked thinner than usual and her eyes larger and keener. She did not seem to notice anyone. Miriam wondered whether she were thinking about cancer. Her face looked as it had done when once or twice she had said, вАЬIch bin so bange vor Krebs.вАЭ She hoped not. Perhaps it was the problem of evil. Perhaps she had thought of it when she put the irises on the table.

She gazed at them, half-feeling the flummery petals against the palm of her hand. Fr√§ulein seemed cancelled. There was no need to feel self-conscious. She was not thinking of any of them. Miriam found herself looking at high grey stone basins, with ornamental stems like wine glasses and large square fluted pedestals, filled with geraniums and calceolarias. They had stood in the sunshine at the corners of the lawn in her grandmotherвАЩs garden. She could remember nothing else but the scent of a greenhouse and its steamy panes over her headвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ lemon thyme and scented geranium.

How lovely it would be today at the end of the day. Fr√§ulein would feel happy thenвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ or did elderly people fear cancer all the time.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ It was a great mistake. You should leave things to Nature.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ You were more likely to have things if you thought about them. But Fr√§ulein would think and worryвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ alone with herselfвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ with her great dark eyes and bony forehead and thin pale cheeksвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ always alone, and just cancer comingвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I shall be like that one dayвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ an old teacher and cancer coming. It was silly to forget all about it and see GrannyвАЩs calceolarias in the sunвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ all that had to come to an end.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ To forget was like putting off repentance. Those who did not put it off saw when the great waters came, a shining figure coming to them through the flood.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ If they did not they were like the man in a nightcap, his mouth hanging openвБ†вАФno teethвБ†вАФand skinny hands, playing cards on his deathbed.

After bed-making, Fr√§ulein settled a mending party at the window end of the schoolroom table. She sent no emissary but was waiting herself in the schoolroom when they came down. She hovered about putting them into their places and enquiring about the work of each one.

She arranged Miriam and the Germans at the Saal end of the table for an English lesson. Mademoiselle was not there. Fr√§ulein herself took the head of the table. Once more she enjoined silenceвБ†вАФthe whole table seemed waiting for Miriam to begin her lesson.

The three or four readings they had done during the term alone in the little room had brought them through about a third of the blue-bound volume. Hoarsely whispering, then violently clearing her throat and speaking suddenly in a very loud tone Miriam bade them resume the story. They read and she corrected them in hoarse whispers. No one appeared to be noticing. A steady breeze coming through the open door of the summerhouse flowed past them and along the table, but Miriam sat stifling, with beating temples. She had no thoughts. Now and again in correcting a simple word she was not sure that she had given the right English rendering. Behind her distress two impressions went to and froвБ†вАФFr√§ulein and the raccommodage party sitting in judgment and the whole roomful waiting for cancer.

Very gently at the end of half an hour Fr√§ulein dismissed the Germans to practise.

Herr Schraub was coming at eleven. Miriam supposed she was free until then and went upstairs.

On the landing she met Mademoiselle coming downstairs with mending.

вАЬBossy coming?вАЭ she said feverishly in French; вАЬare you going to the Saal?вАЭ

Mademoiselle stood contemplating her.

вАЬIвАЩve just been giving an English lesson, oh, Mon Dieu,вАЭ she proceeded.

Mademoiselle still looked gravely and quietly.

Miriam was passing on. Mademoiselle turned and said hurriedly in a low voice. вАЬElsa says you are a fool at lessons.вАЭ

вАЬOh,вАЭ smiled Miriam.

вАЬYou think they do not speak of you, hein? Well, I tell you they speak of you. Jimmie says you are as fat as any German. She laughed in saying that. Gertrude, too, thinks you are a fool. Oh, they say things. If I should tell you all the things they say you would not believe.вАЭ

вАЬI dare say,вАЭ said Miriam heavily, moving on.

вАЬEveryone, all say things, I tell you,вАЭ whispered Mademoiselle turning her head as she went on downstairs.

Miriam ran into the empty summerhouse tearing open a well-filled envelope. There was a long letter from Eve, a folded half sheet from mother. Her heart beat rapidly. Thick straight rain was seething down into the garden.

вАЬCome and say goodbye to Mademoiselle, Hendy.вАЭ

вАЬIs she going?вАЭ

вАЬUmph.вАЭ

вАЬLittle Mademoiselle?вАЭ

вАЬPoor little beast!вАЭ

вАЬLeaving!вАЭ

вАЬSeems like itвБ†вАФsheвАЩs been packing all the morning.вАЭ

вАЬBecause of that letter business?вАЭ

вАЬOh, I dunno. Anyhow thereвАЩs some story of some friend of Fr√§uleinвАЩs travelling through to Besan√Іon today and MademoiselleвАЩs going with her and weвАЩre all to take solemn leave and sheвАЩs not coming back next term. Come on.вАЭ

Mademoiselle, radiantly rosy under her large black French hat, wearing her stockinette jacket and grey dress, was standing at the end of the schoolroom tableвБ†вАФthe girls were all assembled and the door into the hall was open.

The housekeeper was laughing and shouting and imitating the puffing of a train. Mademoiselle stood smiling beside her with downcast eyes.

Opposite them was Gertrude with thin white face, blue lips and hotly blazing eyes fixed on Mademoiselle. She stood easily with her hands clasped behind her.

She must have an appalling headache thought Miriam. Mademoiselle began shaking hands.

вАЬI say, Mademoiselle,вАЭ began Jimmie quietly and hurriedly in her lame French, as she took her hand. вАЬHave you got another place?вАЭ

вАЬA place?вАЭ

вАЬI mean what are you going to do next term, petite?вАЭ

вАЬNext term?вАЭ

вАЬWe want to know about your plans.вАЭ

вАЬBut I remain now with my parents till my marriage!вАЭ

вАЬPetite!!! Fancy never telling us.вАЭ

Exclamations clustered round from all over the room.

вАЬWhy should I tell?вАЭ

вАЬWe didnвАЩt even know you were engaged!вАЭ

вАЬBut of course. Certainly I marry. I know quite well who is to marry me.вАЭ

The room was taking leave of Mademoiselle almost in silence. The English were standing together. Miriam heard their voices. вАЬвАЩDieu, mвАЩselle, вАЩdieu, mвАЩselle,вАЭ one after the other and saw hands and wrists move vigorously up and down. The Germans were commenting, вАЬAh, she is engagedвБ†вАФah, whatвБ†вАФenвАСgaged. Ah, the rascal! H√ґr malвБ†вАФвАЭ

Miriam dreaded her turn. Mademoiselle was coming nearвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ so cheap and common-looking with her hard grey dress and her cheap jacket with the hat hiding her hair and making her look skinny and old. She was a more dreadful stranger than she had been at firstвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Miriam wished she could stay. She could not let anyone go away like this. They would not meet again and Mademoiselle was going away detesting her and them all, going away in disgrace and not minding and going to be married. All the time there had been that waiting for her. She was smiling now and showing her babyish teeth. How could Jimmie hold her by the shoulders?

вАЬVenez mon enfant, venez √† lвАЩinstant,вАЭ called Fr√§ulein from the hall.

Mademoiselle made her hard little sound with her throat.

вАЬWhy doesnвАЩt she go?вАЭ thought Miriam as Mademoiselle ran down the room. вАЬAdieu, adieu evaireeboddieвБ†вАФallaвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬAre all here?вАЭ

Jimmie answered and Fr√§ulein came to the table and stood leaning for a moment upon one hand.

The door opened and the housekeeper shone hard and bright in the doorway.

вАЬW√§sche angekommen!вАЭ

вАЬNa, gut,вАЭ responded Fr√§ulein quietly.

The housekeeper disappeared.

вАЬFr√§ulein looks like a dead body,вАЭ thought Miriam.

Apprehension overtook herвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬthereвАЩs going to be some silly fuss.вАЭ

вАЬI shall speak in English, because the most that I shall say concerns the English members of this household and its heavy seriousness will be by those who are not English, sufficiently understood.вАЭ

Miriam flushed, struggling for self-possession. She determined not to listen.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬDamnвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ DevilвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ she exhorted herselfвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬhumbugging creatureвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ She felt the blood throbbing in her face and her eyes and looked at no one. She was conscious that little movements and sounds came from the Germans, but she heard nothing but Fr√§uleinвАЩs voice which had ceased. It had been the clear-cut low-breathing tone she used at prayers. вАЬOh, Lord, bother, damnation,вАЭ she reiterated in her discomfiture. The words echoing through her mind seemed to cut a way of escape.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

вАЬThat dear child,вАЭ smiled Fr√§uleinвАЩs voice, вАЬwho has just left us, came under this roofвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ nearly a year ago.

вАЬShe came, a tender girl (MademoiselleвБ†вАФMademoiselle, oh, goodness!) from the house of her pious parents, fromme Eltern, fromme Eltern.вАЭ Fr√§ulein breathed these words slowly out and a deep sigh came from one of the Germans, вАЬto reside with us. She came in the most perfect confidence with the aim to complete her own simple education, the pious and simple nurture of a Protestant French girl, and with the aim also to remove for a period something of the burden lying upon the shoulders of those dear parents in the upbringing of herself and her brothers and sisters.вАЭ (And then to leave home and be marriedвБ†вАФhow easy, how easy!)

вАЬHonourablyвБ†вАФhonourably she has fulfilled each and every duty laid upon her as institutrice in this establishment.

вАЬSufficient to indicate this fulfilment of duty is the fact that she was happy and that she made happy othersвБ†вАФвАЭ

Fr√§uleinвАЩs voice dropped to its lowest note and grew fuller in tone.

вАЬWould that I could here complete what I have to say of the sojourn of little Aline Ducorroy under this roof.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But that I cannot do.

вАЬThat I cannot do.

вАЬIt has been the experience of this pure and gentle soul to come, under this roof, in contact with things not pure.вАЭ

Fr√§uleinвАЩs voice had become breathless and shaking. Both her hands sought the support of the table.

вАЬThis poor child has had unwillingly to suffer the fact of associating with those not pure.вАЭ

вАЬAch, Fr√§ulein! What you say!вАЭ ejaculated Clara.

In the silence the leaves of the chestnut tree tapped one against the other. Miriam listened to themвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ there must be a little breeze blowing across the garden. Why had she not noticed it before? Were they all hearing it?

вАЬWithвБ†вАФthoseвБ†вАФnot pure.вАЭ

вАЬHere, in this my school.вАЭ

MiriamвАЩs heart began to beat angrily.

вАЬShe has been forced, here, in this school, to hear talkingвАЭвБ†вАФFr√§uleinвАЩs voice thickenedвБ†вАФвАЬof men.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬM√§nnerвАСgeschichtenвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ here!вАЭ

вАЬM√§nnerвАСgeschichten.вАЭ Fr√§uleinвАЩs voice rang out down the table. She bent forward so that the light from both the windows behind her fell sharply across her grey-clad shoulders and along the top of her head. There was no condemnation Miriam felt in those broad grey shouldersвБ†вАФthey were innocent. But the head shining and flat, the wide parting, the sleekness of the hair falling thinly and flatly away from itвБ†вАФangry, dreadful skull. She writhed away from it. She would not look any more. She felt her neck was swelling inside her collar band.

Fr√§ulein whispered low.

вАЬHere in my school, here standing round this table are those who talk ofвБ†вАФmen.

вАЬYoung girlsвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ who talkвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ of men.вАЭ

While Fr√§ulein waited, trembling, several of the girls began to snuffle and sob.

вАЬIs there, can there be in the world anything that is more base, more vile, more impure? Is there? Is there?вАЭ

Miriam wished she knew who was crying. She tried to fix her thoughts on a hole in the table cover. вАЬIt could be darned.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ It could be darned.вАЭ

вАЬYou are brought here together, each and all of you here together in the time of your youth. It is, it should be for you the most beautiful occasion. Can you find anything more terrible than that such occasion where all may work and influence each otherвБ†вАФfor all lifeвБ†вАФin purity and goodnessвБ†вАФthat such occasion should be usedвБ†вАФimpurely? Like a dawn, like a dawn for purity should be the life of a maiden. Calm, and pure and with holy prayer.вАЭ

Miriam repeated these words in her mind trying to dwell on the beauty of Fr√§uleinвАЩs middle tones. вАЬAnd the day shall come, I shall wish, for all of you, that the sanctity of a home shall be within your hands. What then shall be the shame, what the regret of those who before the coming of that sacred time did think thoughts of men, did speak of them? Shame, shame,вАЭ whispered Fr√§ulein amidst the sobbing of the girls.

вАЬWith the thoughts of those who have this impure nature I can do nothing. For them it is freely to acknowledge this evil in the heart and to pray that the heart may be changed and made clean.

вАЬBut a thing I can do and I do.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I will have no more of this talking. In my school I will have no more.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Do you hear, all? Do you hear?вАЭ

She struck the table with both fists and brandished them in midair.

вАЬEhвАСh,вАЭ she sneered. вАЬI know, I know who are the culprits. I have always known.вАЭ She gasped. вАЬIt shall ceaseвБ†вАФthese talksвБ†вАФthis vile talk of men. Do you understand? It shall cease. IвБ†вАФwillвБ†вАФnotвБ†вАФhaveвБ†вАФit.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ The school shall be cleanвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ from pupil to pupilвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ from room to room.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Every dayвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ every hour.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Shameless!вАЭ she screamed. вАЬShameless. Ah! I know. I know you.вАЭ She stood with her arms folded, swaying, and gave a little laugh. вАЬYou think to deceive me. You do not deceive me. I know. I have known and I shall know. This school is mine. Mine! My place! I will have it as I will have it. That is clear and plain, and you all shall help me. I shall say no more. But I shall know what to do.вАЭ

Mechanically Miriam went downstairs with the rest of the party. With the full force of her nerves she resisted the echoes of Fr√§uleinвАЩs onslaught, refusing to think of anything she had said and blotting out her image every time it rose. The essential was that she would be dismissed as Mademoiselle had been dismissed. That was the upshot of it all for her. Fr√§ulein was a mad, silly, pious female who would send her away and go on glowering over the Bible. She would have to go, go, go in a sort of disgrace.

The girls were talking all round her, excitedly. She despised them for showing that they were disturbed by Fr√§uleinвАЩs despotic nonsense. As they reached the basement she remembered the letter crushed in her hand and sat down on the last step to glance through it.

вАЬDearest Mim. I have a wonderful piece of news for you. I wonder what you will say? It is about Harriett. She has asked me to tell you as she does not like to write about it herself.вАЭ

With steady hands Miriam turned the closely-written sheets reading a phrase here and thereвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬregularly in the seat behind us at All SaintsвАЩ for monthsвБ†вАФsaw her with the Pooles at a concert at the Assembly Rooms and made up his mind thenвБ†вАФthe moment he saw herвБ†вАФjoined the tennis clubвБ†вАФthey won the double handicapвБ†вАФa beautiful Slazenger racquetвБ†вАФonly just over sixteenвБ†вАФfor yearsвБ†вАФof course Mother says itвАЩs just a little foolish nonsenseвБ†вАФbut I am not sure that she really thinks soвБ†вАФGerald took me into his confidenceвБ†вАФmade a solemn callвБ†вАФadmirably suited to each otherвБ†вАФrather a long melancholy good-looking faceвБ†вАФthey look such a contrastвБ†вАФthe big Canadian RailwayвБ†вАФnot exactly a clerkвБ†вАФsomething rather above that, to do with making drafts of things and so on. Very sweet and charmingвБ†вАФmy own young daysвБ†вАФthat I have reached the great age of twenty-threeвБ†вАФresident post in the countryвБ†вАФtwo little girlsвБ†вАФwe think it very good payвБ†вАФI shall go in SeptemberвБ†вАФplenty of timeвБ†вАФthat you should come home for the long holidays. We are all looking forward to itвБ†вАФthe tennis clubвБ†вАФyour name as a holiday memberвБ†вАФthe American tournament in AugustвБ†вАФHarry was the youngest lady member like youвБ†вАФof course Harry could not let you come without knowingвБ†вАФfind somebody travelling throughвБ†вАФFr√§ulein PfaffвБ†вАФexpect to see you looking like a flour sack with a string tied round its waistвБ†вАФall the dwarf roses in bloomвБ†вАФhardly any strawberriesвБ†вАФwe shall see you soonвБ†вАФeverybody sends.вАЭ

Miriam got up and swung the half-read letter above her head like a dumbbell.

She looked about her like a strangerвБ†вАФeverything was as it had been the day she cameвБ†вАФthe little cramped basement hallвБ†вАФthe strange German girlsвБ†вАФsmall and old looking, poking about amongst the baskets. She hardly knew them. She passed half-blindly amongst them with her eyes wide. The little dressing room seemed full of bright light. She saw everyone at once clearly. All the English girls were there. She knew every line of each of them. They were her old friends. They knew her. Looking at none of them she felt she embraced them all, closely, and that they knew it. They shone. They were beautiful. She wanted to cry aloud. She was English and free. She had nothing to do with this German school. Baskets at her feet made her pick her way. Solomon was kneeling at one, sorting and handing out. At a little table under the window Millie stood jotting pencil notes in a pocketbook. Judy was at her side. The others were grouped about the piano. Gertrude sat on the keyboard her legs dangling.

Miriam plumped down on a full basket.

вАЬHullo, Hendy, old chap, you look all right!вАЭ

Miriam looked fearlessly up at the faces that were turned towards her. Again she seemed to see all of them at once. The circle of her vision seemed huge. It was as if the confining rim of her glasses were gone and she saw equally from eyes that seemed to fill her face. She drew all their eyes to her. They were waiting for her to speak. For a moment it seemed as if they stood there lifeless. She had drawn all their meaning and all their happiness into herself. She could do as she wished with themвБ†вАФtheir poor little lives.

They stood waiting for some word from her. She dropped her eyes and caught the flash of GertrudeвАЩs swinging steel buckles.

вАЬWasnвАЩt Fr√§ulein angry?вАЭ she said carelessly.

Someone pushed the door to.

вАЬSly old bird.вАЭ

вАЬFancy imagining we shouldnвАЩt see through Mademoiselle leaving.вАЭ

вАЬHвАЩm,вАЭ said Miriam.

вАЬI knew Mademoiselle would sneak if she had half a chance.вАЭ

вАЬYes, ever since she got so thick with Elsa.вАЭ

вАЬOh!вБ†вАФElsa.вАЭ

вАЬYou bet Fr√§ulein looks down on the two of them in her heart of hearts.вАЭ

вАЬMвАЩmвБ†вАФsheвАЩs fairly sick, Jemima, with the lot of us this time.вАЭ

вАЬMademoiselle told her some pretty things,вАЭ laughed Gertrude. вАЬLily thinks weвАЩre lost soulsвБ†вАФnearly all of us.вАЭ

вАЬOnny swaw, my dears, onny swaw.вАЭ

вАЬItвАЩs all very well. But thereвАЩs no knowing what Mademoiselle would make her believe. SheвАЩd got reams about you, HendyвБ†вАФnothing bad enough.вАЭ

вАЬHвАЩm,вАЭ said Miriam, вАЬI can imagineвБ†вАФвАЭ

Her thoughts brought back a day when she had shown Mademoiselle the names in her birthday book and dwelt on one page and let Mademoiselle understand that it was the pageвБ†вАФbrown eyesвБ†вАФles yeux brunes fonc√©s. Why did Mademoiselle and Fr√§ulein think that badвБ†вАФwant to spoil it for her? She had said nothing about the confidences of the German girls to anyone. Elsa must have found that out from Clara.

вАЬOh, well itвАЩs all over now. LetвАЩs be thankful and think no more about it.вАЭ

вАЬAll very fine, Jemima. YouвАЩre going home.вАЭ

вАЬThank goodness.вАЭ

вАЬAnd not coming back. Lucky Pigleinchen.вАЭ

вАЬWell, so am I,вАЭ said Miriam, вАЬand IвАЩm not coming back.вАЭ

вАЬI say! ArenвАЩt you coming to Norderney?вАЭ Gertrude flashed dark eyes at her.

вАЬCanвАЩt you come to Norderney?вАЭ said Judy thickly, at her elbow.

вАЬWell, you see there are all sorts of things happening at home. I must go. One of my sisters is engaged and another going away. I must go home for a while. Of course I might come back.вАЭ

вАЬThink it over, Henderson, and see if you canвАЩt decide in our favour.вАЭ

вАЬWe shall have another Miss Owen.вАЭ

Miriam struggled up out of her basket. вАЬBut I thought you all liked Miss Owen!вАЭ

вАЬHo! Goodness! Too simple for words.вАЭ

вАЬYou never told us you had any sisters, Hendy,вАЭ said Jimmie, tapping her on the wrist.

вАЬWhat a pity youвАЩre going just as weвАЩre getting to know you,вАЭ Judy smiled shyly and looked on the floor.

вАЬWellвБ†вАФIвАЩm off with my bundle,вАЭ announced Gertrude. вАЬTo be continued in our next. Think it over, Hendy. DonвАЩt desert us. Hurry up, my room. ItвАЩll be teatime before weвАЩre straight. Come on, Jim.вАЭ

Miriam moved, with Judy following at her elbow, across the room to Millie. She looked up with her little plaintive frown. Miriam could not remember what her plans were. вАЬLetвАЩs see,вАЭ she said, вАЬyouвАЩre going to Norderney, arenвАЩt you?вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩm not going to Norderney,вАЭ said Millie almost tearfully. вАЬI only wish I were. I donвАЩt even know IвАЩm coming back next term.вАЭ

вАЬArenвАЩt you looking forward to the holidays?вАЭ

вАЬI donвАЩt know. IвАЩd rather be staying here if IвАЩm not coming back after.вАЭ

вАЬTo stay in Germany? YouвАЩd rather do that than anything?вАЭ

вАЬRather.вАЭ

вАЬHere, with Fr√§ulein Pfaff?вАЭ

вАЬOf course, here with Fr√§ulein Pfaff. IвАЩd rather be in Germany than anything.вАЭ

Millie stood staring with her pout and her slightly raised eyebrows at the frosted window.

вАЬWould you stay here in the school for the holidays if Fr√§ulein were staying?вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩd do anything,вАЭ said Millie, вАЬto stay in Germany.вАЭ

вАЬYou know,вАЭ said Miriam gazing at her, вАЬso would IвБ†вАФany mortal thing.вАЭ

MillieвАЩs eyes had filled with tears.

вАЬThen why donвАЩt ye stay?вАЭ said Judy, with gentle gruffness.

The house was shut up for the night.

Miriam looked up at the clock dizzily as she drank the last of her coffee. It marked half past eleven. Fr√§ulein had told her to be ready at a quarter to twelve. Her hands felt large and shaky and her feet were cold. The room was stiflingвБ†вАФbare and brown in the gaslight. She left it and crept through the hall where her trunk stood and up the creaking stairs. She turned up the gas. Emma lay asleep with red eyelids and cheeks. Miriam did not look at Ulrica. Hurriedly and desolately she packed her bag. She was going home empty-handed. She had achieved nothing. Fr√§ulein had made not the slightest effort to keep her. She was just nothing againвБ†вАФwith her Saratoga trunk and her handbag. Harriett had achieved. Harriett. She was just going home with nothing to say for herself.

вАЬThe carriage is here, my child. Make haste.вАЭ

Miriam pushed things hurriedly into her bag. Fr√§ulein had gone downstairs.

She was ready. She looked numbly round the room. Emma looked very far away. She turned out the gas. The dim light from the landing shone into the room. She stood for a moment in the doorway looking back. The room seemed to be empty. There seemed to be nothing in it but the black screen standing round the bed that was no longer hers.

вАЬGoodbye,вАЭ she murmured and hurried downstairs.

In the hall Fr√§ulein began to talk at once, talking until they were seated side by side in the dark cab.

Then Miriam gazed freely at the pale profile shining at her side. Poor Fr√§ulein Pfaff, getting old.

Fr√§ulein began to ask about MiriamвАЩs plans for the future. Miriam answered as to an equal, elaborating a little account of circumstances at home, and the doings of her sisters. As she spoke she felt that Fr√§ulein envied her her youth and her family at home in EnglandвБ†вАФand she raised her voice a little and laughed easily and moved, crossing her knees in the cab.

She used sentimental German words about HarriettвБ†вАФa description of her that might have applied to EmmaвБ†вАФlittle emphatic tender epithets came to her from the conversations of the girls. Fr√§ulein praised her German warmly and asked question after question about the house and garden at Barnes and presently of her mother.

вАЬI canвАЩt talk about her,вАЭ said Miriam shortly.

вАЬThat is English,вАЭ murmured Fr√§ulein.

вАЬSheвАЩs such a little thing,вАЭ said Miriam, вАЬsmaller than any of us.вАЭ Presently Fr√§ulein laid her gloved hand on MiriamвАЩs gloved one. вАЬYou and I have, I think, much in common.вАЭ

Miriam frozeвБ†вАФand looked at the gas lamps slowly swinging by along the boulevard. вАЬMuch will have happened in England whilst you have been here with us,вАЭ said Fr√§ulein eagerly.

They reached a streetвБ†вАФshuttered darkness where the shops were, and here and there the yellow flare of a caf√©. She strained her eyes to see the faces and forms of men and womenвБ†вАФbreathing more quickly as she watched the characteristic German gait.

There was the station.

Her trunk was weighed and registered. There was something to pay. She handed her purse to Fr√§ulein and stood gazing at the uniformed manвБ†вАФruddy and clear-eyedвБ†вАФclear hard blue eyes and hard clean clear yellow moustachesвБ†вАФdecisive untroubled movements. Passengers were walking briskly about and laughing and shouting remarks to each other. The train stood waiting for her. The ringing of an enormous bell brought her hands to her ears. Fr√§ulein gently propelled her up the three steps into a compartment marked Damen-Coup√©. It smelt of biscuits and wine.

A man with a booming voice came to examine her ticket. He stood bending under the central light, uttering sturdy German words. Miriam drank them in without understanding. He left the carriage very empty. The great bell was ringing again. Fr√§ulein standing on the top step pressed both her hands and murmured words of farewell.

вАЬLebвАЩ wohl, mein Kind, Gott segne dich.вАЭ

вАЬGoodbye, Fr√§ulein,вАЭ she said stiffly, shaking hands.

The door was shut with a slamвБ†вАФthe light seemed to go down. Miriam glanced at itвБ†вАФhalf the dull green muslin shade had slipped over the gas globe. The carriage seemed dark. The platform outside was very bright. Fr√§ulein had disappeared. The train was high above the platform. Politely smiling Miriam scrambled to the window. The platform was moving, the large bright station moving away. Fr√§uleinвАЩs wide smile was creasing and caverning under her hat from which the veil was thrown back.

Standing at the window Miriam smiled sharply. Fr√§uleinвАЩs form flowed slowly away with the platform.

Groups passed by smiling and waving.

Miriam sat down.

She leaped up to lean from the window.

The platform had disappeared.
